title: Tell me about a time you had a conflict with a coworker. How did you resolve it?

Tutor

All Questions
Were You Asked This?
Tell me about a time you had a conflict with a coworker. How did you resolve it?

Asked at:

DoorDash

Meta

Trustpilot

Google

+22 More
Try This Question Yourself

Practice with feedback and follow-up questions

Start Practice
What is this question about

Interviewers use this question to assess whether you handle disagreement in a mature, productive way rather than avoiding it, escalating too quickly, or trying to 'win.' They want to understand how you interpret other people's motives, whether you can separate the person from the problem, and how you move a situation toward resolution. At higher levels, they also look for whether you can resolve conflict in a way that improves team effectiveness, not just gets past one awkward moment.

Also asked as...
Conflict Resolution
Communication
Ownership
Leadership
Breakdown for "Tell me about a time you had a conflict with a coworker. How did you resolve it?" starts at 2:09
Key Insights
Conflict does not have to mean an argument. Strong answers often involve differences in priorities, risk tolerance, quality standards, or ownership boundaries; naming those underlying tensions shows maturity.
You should show your own role in the conflict clearly. Honest ownership of how you contributed, even in a small way, is a strong signal; answers that make the other person sound unreasonable throughout usually read as low self-awareness.
Do not end the story at 'we talked.' Explain what changed after the conversation: a decision, a working agreement, a better relationship, or a repeatable process that reduced future friction.
What interviewers probe at
level
Top Priority
Did the candidate show appropriate ownership rather than blame, avoidance, or over-reliance on escalation?

You are not expected to solve everything alone, but you should show that you tried to engage directly before handing the problem upward.

Good examples

🟢
I first talked with them one-on-one to make sure I understood the disagreement, and only later asked my lead for advice on the tradeoff.

🟢
I owned that I had pushed my concern in the group setting too bluntly, so I reset the conversation privately and focused on the issue rather than proving my point.

Bad examples

🔴
They would not listen, so I immediately asked my manager to step in and tell them the right answer.

🔴
It was mostly their fault because they were not considering the edge cases I pointed out.

Weak answers externalize responsibility; strong answers show initiative, self-awareness, and proportionate use of support.

Did the candidate try to understand the other person's perspective and constraints before pushing their own solution?
Did the candidate work toward a resolution that balanced progress, relationship health, and the underlying problem?
Valuable
Did the candidate choose a conflict that is meaningful enough to reveal judgment and interpersonal skill?
Did the resolution lead to a credible outcome or lasting change in how the candidate worked with others?
Example answers at
level
Great answers

On a small bug fix, a teammate and I disagreed about whether to patch the symptom quickly or spend a little more time fixing the underlying validation issue. At first I was pushing for the more complete fix because I was worried we'd see the bug again, but when I talked to them one-on-one, I learned another teammate was blocked waiting for this change and they were trying to unblock that work the same day. We agreed to do a focused check on the risky part first, and when that showed the larger fix would take longer than expected, we shipped the safe patch with a follow-up task already assigned. I documented the edge cases so the temporary solution was clear, and we closed the follow-up the next sprint. That experience taught me to understand what pressure the other person is under before arguing for the technically nicer option. After that, I got better at framing tradeoffs instead of just saying which solution I preferred.

In my first year on the team I had a conflict with a coworker after a code review where they left a blunt comment calling my approach “wrong,” and I took it personally. Instead of replying in the thread, I asked for a 15-minute meeting to talk through the concerns so we could avoid misunderstanding. On the call I learned they were rushed and trying to be efficient rather than attacking my skills, and I explained how the tone felt discouraging; we agreed on a simple feedback format (describe the issue, explain why, and suggest a concrete fix) and that either of us could request a short sync if a review felt confrontational. We also added a quick checklist for PRs so expectations were clearer up front. That change reduced friction in later reviews and taught me to address tone and process early instead of letting resentment build.

Poor answers

I had a conflict with a coworker about how to implement a bug fix because they wanted a workaround and I wanted the correct solution. I explained why my way was better, but they kept pushing back, so I asked our lead and he agreed with me. We implemented my approach and the issue was fixed. It showed me that if you have the stronger technical argument, it's best to be direct and get a decision quickly.

Yes, I was asked the conflict resolution for different scenarios and the real time experiences that I shared were not enough I suppose. They were looking for some complicated and juicy scenarios as per the feedback I received

3

Reply
B
BottomTealSkunk679
Premium
• 8个月前

Can you share which company gave this feedback? I got feedback that they wanted something simple, and I think juicy, and mine was too complicated (for Meta E5). I think spoke too long on this one question and I should have picked something simpler

0

Reply
F
FullOrangeMammal512
Premium
• 8个月前

Any suggestions community can provide on the below answer. is the response looking too technical? Also is it giving the signal that interviewer is looking for E5 roles at meta?

We received a high-priority request from our Data Governance team to bring in product metadata from one of our internal systems so they could build executive-facing dashboards. This data would help senior leadership make decisions around product certifications and compliance, so it was important to deliver something fast, reliable, and scalable.

I took ownership of the data pipeline that would bring this information into our analytics environment. There were two ways to access the data — and early on, I recommended one method, while a colleague from another team strongly preferred another. We had a disagreement on which approach to use.

My goal was to make a decision that would serve both the immediate business need and be sustainable as usage scaled. At the same time, it was important to find alignment with my colleague, since we were jointly responsible for the long-term reliability of the platform.

I didn’t want this to turn into a “my way vs. yours” situation. I scheduled time with my colleague to fully understand his reasoning. He was leaning toward a familiar method that was simpler to set up and had worked well in the past.

I shared my concerns with that option: while easy upfront, it would require us to pull the full dataset — millions of records — every time. That meant longer processing times, higher cloud costs, and slower data availability for leadership reports.

The method I suggested allowed us to bring in just the new or changed data, making it more efficient and cost-effective. I also acknowledged that it had some complexity, and I took responsibility for handling that part myself, so it wouldn’t create extra work for others.

Throughout the conversation, I kept the focus on what mattered most: delivering accurate data on time for the executive team, without creating future bottlenecks. Once we reviewed the full picture, we agreed to move forward with the more scalable option.

R – Result
We delivered the data ahead of schedule, which allowed the governance team to launch the executive dashboards on time.
By choosing a more efficient approach, we reduced the time and resources required by around 40%, which saved costs and improved reliability.

Our collaboration set a positive tone for future cross-team efforts — and the solution we built has since been reused in other projects as a model for handling similar data needs.

2

Reply
F
FascinatingAmethystMarsupial369
Top 1%
• 7个月前

Try to use LLMs, they are pretty helpful in fleshing out your story. This is a response from Claude:

Is it too technical?
No, it's actually not technical enough for a senior engineering role. You mention concepts like "data pipeline" and "incremental vs full loads," but these are fairly high-level. For E5, interviewers often want to see:

Specific technical trade-offs you evaluated
Why the incremental approach was architecturally better (not just "faster")
Technical risks you mitigated
System design considerations

Does it signal E5?
Partially, but could be stronger. Here's what works and what's missing:
✅ E5 Signals Present:

Business impact awareness (exec dashboards, compliance)
Cross-team collaboration
Long-term thinking (scalability, reusability)
Quantified results (40% efficiency gain)

⚠️ Missing E5 Signals:

Scope feels limited - One pipeline with one colleague. E5s typically influence multiple teams/projects
"I handled the complexity myself" - This sounds IC-level. E5s should show:

How you enabled others
Built reusable frameworks
Influenced team practices

Shallow technical depth - What were the actual trade-offs? Cost vs complexity? Latency requirements? Data consistency models?
Limited strategic impact - How did this change the way your org approaches similar problems?

Specific Suggestions:
Add more technical substance:

"We evaluated batch vs CDC (Change Data Capture). The batch approach would require 6-hour processing windows, risking SLA misses. CDC gave us near-real-time updates with 95% lower compute costs."

Elevate the scope:

"This became a pattern we documented for 8 other teams facing similar integration challenges"
"I led a design review with 3 teams to establish this as our standard approach"

Show leadership, not just execution:

Instead of "I took responsibility for handling complexity"
Try: "I created a reusable framework and paired with two engineers to build shared expertise"

0

Reply
P
PeacefulJadeCarp445
Premium
• 7个月前

For this one - is it okay to talk about a conflict with a customer?

0

Reply

Stefan Mai

Admin
• 1个月前

I would be very careful with this. Many companies (e.g. Amazon) consider customers to be sacrosanct and if it seems like you aren't holding them in the highest regard this could be considered a flag. There may be some valuable stories here, but honestly I'd advise choosing a different situation unless it's the only one you have.

1

Reply

Any suggestions community can provide on the below answer. is the response looking too technical? Also is it giving the signal that interviewer is looking for E5 roles at meta?

We received a high-priority request from our Data Governance team to bring in product metadata from one of our internal systems so they could build executive-facing dashboards. This data would help senior leadership make decisions around product certifications and compliance, so it was important to deliver something fast, reliable, and scalable.

I took ownership of the data pipeline that would bring this information into our analytics environment. There were two ways to access the data — and early on, I recommended one method, while a colleague from another team strongly preferred another. We had a disagreement on which approach to use.

My goal was to make a decision that would serve both the immediate business need and be sustainable as usage scaled. At the same time, it was important to find alignment with my colleague, since we were jointly responsible for the long-term reliability of the platform.

I didn’t want this to turn into a “my way vs. yours” situation. I scheduled time with my colleague to fully understand his reasoning. He was leaning toward a familiar method that was simpler to set up and had worked well in the past.

I shared my concerns with that option: while easy upfront, it would require us to pull the full dataset — millions of records — every time. That meant longer processing times, higher cloud costs, and slower data availability for leadership reports.

The method I suggested allowed us to bring in just the new or changed data, making it more efficient and cost-effective. I also acknowledged that it had some complexity, and I took responsibility for handling that part myself, so it wouldn’t create extra work for others.

Throughout the conversation, I kept the focus on what mattered most: delivering accurate data on time for the executive team, without creating future bottlenecks. Once we reviewed the full picture, we agreed to move forward with the more scalable option.

R – Result
We delivered the data ahead of schedule, which allowed the governance team to launch the executive dashboards on time.
By choosing a more efficient approach, we reduced the time and resources required by around 40%, which saved costs and improved reliability.

Our collaboration set a positive tone for future cross-team efforts — and the solution we built has since been reused in other projects as a model for handling similar data needs.

Try to use LLMs, they are pretty helpful in fleshing out your story. This is a response from Claude:

Is it too technical?
No, it's actually not technical enough for a senior engineering role. You mention concepts like "data pipeline" and "incremental vs full loads," but these are fairly high-level. For E5, interviewers often want to see:

Specific technical trade-offs you evaluated
Why the incremental approach was architecturally better (not just "faster")
Technical risks you mitigated
System design considerations

Does it signal E5?
Partially, but could be stronger. Here's what works and what's missing:
✅ E5 Signals Present:

Business impact awareness (exec dashboards, compliance)
Cross-team collaboration
Long-term thinking (scalability, reusability)
Quantified results (40% efficiency gain)

⚠️ Missing E5 Signals:

Scope feels limited - One pipeline with one colleague. E5s typically influence multiple teams/projects
"I handled the complexity myself" - This sounds IC-level. E5s should show:

How you enabled others
Built reusable frameworks
Influenced team practices

Shallow technical depth - What were the actual trade-offs? Cost vs complexity? Latency requirements? Data consistency models?
Limited strategic impact - How did this change the way your org approaches similar problems?

Specific Suggestions:
Add more technical substance:

"We evaluated batch vs CDC (Change Data Capture). The batch approach would require 6-hour processing windows, risking SLA misses. CDC gave us near-real-time updates with 95% lower compute costs."

Elevate the scope:

"This became a pattern we documented for 8 other teams facing similar integration challenges"
"I led a design review with 3 teams to establish this as our standard approach"

Show leadership, not just execution:

Instead of "I took responsibility for handling complexity"
Try: "I created a reusable framework and paired with two engineers to build shared expertise"

I would be very careful with this. Many companies (e.g. Amazon) consider customers to be sacrosanct and if it seems like you aren't holding them in the highest regard this could be considered a flag. There may be some valuable stories here, but honestly I'd advise choosing a different situation unless it's the only one you have.
